text: Look closely at the file ownership: `ls -la /home/reports/data`. If the owner column shows a plain number instead of a username, that UID doesn't match any current account -- a strong sign an account's UID changed underneath its own files.
Check what UID the `reports` account actually has right now: `id reports`. Compare that number against the numeric owner you saw on the files.

They don't match. The account was recreated (e.g. from a backup/provisioning tool) and came back with a different UID than before, while the files on disk still carry the old one -- Linux permissions are entirely UID-number based, not name-based, so as far as the kernel is concerned, `reports` and the owner of those files are now two completely different identities.

The files' UID is the source of truth here (that's the data). Reassign the account back to match it: `sudo usermod -u <old-uid> reports`, then confirm `ls -la /home/reports/data` now shows `reports` as the owner again, and the account can read/write its own files.
